data.title: Why do currency-related cases get stuck at the "civil remedy stage"?
data.description:
Introduction: The risks of cryptocurrency-related cases are concentrated in the "relief phase"

When handling cryptocurrency-related cases, you will repeatedly encounter a very typical emotion of the parties involved:

"I know I've been scammed, the money has indeed been transferred, and I can see it on the blockchain, so why is no one taking action and I can't get it back?"

The problem is often not at the factual level, but at the procedural level—

The clearer the facts, the easier it is for the parties involved to mistakenly believe that "relief can be initiated"; however, in cryptocurrency-related cases, whether relief can be initiated depends on three things: the nature of the crime, jurisdiction, and whether the evidence can be put into practice.

The nature of cryptocurrency disputes has changed in recent years: Early on, it was mostly about "theft and absconding"; now, it's more about seemingly legitimate structures (listing services, U-merchant exchanges, NFT investment platforms), but with more complex asset paths, more dispersed entities, and more frequent cross-border involvement. Therefore, when cases reach the "relief stage," they often encounter three obstacles: The assets have value, but their nature and transaction relationships are not easily determined quickly: Is it property, contractual subject matter, investment rights, or a "tool" in a suspected criminal chain? Cross-border and multi-entity structures make jurisdiction and evidence collection a real challenge: The blockchain is here, the exchange is there, the server is overseas, and the person has absconded? Civil and criminal matters are mutually constrained: Once deemed "potentially involved in criminal activity," civil matters can easily be stalled or even transferred to other jurisdictions, and the pace of redress is completely out of the parties' control. The following three typical cases will clarify "why the process is stalled" and "what a lawyer can do." Real Case Review: Case 1: Cross-border "Listing Service Fee" Scam A company from country H planned to list its token on an exchange in country S and contacted a Chinese salesperson at the exchange. They agreed to pay 800,000 USDT as a listing service fee. After payment, the salesperson disappeared, and the exchange claimed the salesperson had resigned, and the service fee had not been received. Key Obstacles in Case Progression The case involves significant cross-border elements, making it difficult to directly initiate civil remedies; regarding procedural choice, the principle of criminal priority requires resolving the issue of whether to file a case first. The cross-border nature of crypto assets presents practical difficulties for evidence collection and jurisdiction, such as wallet transaction records being distributed across different blockchains and exchange servers located overseas. There is disagreement regarding the nature of the conduct: whether it constitutes criminal fraud or civil breach of contract directly determines which procedure the case proceeds to and whether remedies can be initiated. What can lawyers do? The first step isn't to rush into writing about "how you were scammed," but rather to outline "how the money went": the transfer chain, wallet address, timeline, the other party's identity documents, communication records, and exchange feedback. Prepare two sets of statements simultaneously: For criminal cases: emphasize the leverage of territorial/personal jurisdiction and stress the key fact of "intent to illegally possess" (inducement, fabricated identity/authority, service promises, refusal to fulfill obligations, disappearance, etc.). For civil cases: compress the dispute into "service fee payment—service not fulfilled—unfounded possession by the other party," providing a foundation for subsequent negotiations/asset preservation. Don't treat "exchange replies" as conclusions, but as entry points for evidence: it's common for exchanges to deny responsibility, but lawyers should turn them into clues about "the other party's internal management, authority, and business relationships." Case 2: USDT Exchange Scam An investor met an "investment advisor" online and was recommended to exchange USDT through a U-merchant. The investor transferred over three million yuan to multiple accounts, but the funds never arrived. The U-merchant was subsequently arrested, but since it only provided exchange services and had no direct connection to the upstream fraud ring, the police ultimately terminated the investigation. Key Obstacles in Case Progress First, conduct a "traceability assessment," then discuss the path forward. This assessment is harsh, but necessary: ​​Which accounts can still be frozen? Which entities can be located? What evidence can be used to close the loop?

Break down the "flow of funds" into two parallel tracks:

  • Bank side: Transfer chain, identity of receiving account, destination of funds (whether concentrated, whether short-term dispersed).

  • On-chain side: Whether there are nodes related to aggregation, collection, cross-chain, and exchange entry related to known wallets.

The core variable in these types of cases is often not "whether a lawsuit can be filed/initiated," but "whether asset control can be achieved at key nodes." We will explain the feasibility and risks of each node to clients simultaneously during the process, ensuring that decisions are based on feasibility.

Case 3: NFT Investment Scam A client purchased a series of high-value NFTs through an online platform, which claimed that these NFTs could generate future art dividends and rare digital rights. After the client paid approximately 5 million RMB, the platform suddenly shut down, the website became inaccessible, and the person in charge disappeared. Subsequent investigations revealed that the NFT smart contract code contained a backdoor, allowing assets to be transferred at will. Key Obstacles in Case Progress As a derivative right of digital assets, NFTs possess both investment and trading attributes, and their legal characterization remains highly uncertain under existing rules. The platform's entity and smart contracts are highly anonymous, and their cross-border deployment presents real obstacles to asset tracking, entity identification, and jurisdiction. Even with technical tracking through contract logs, on-chain records, or IP information, cross-chain assets often involve multiple jurisdictions, making actual recovery extremely difficult. Expanding the practical perspective: Translating the technical facts into legal terms: a contract backdoor means control is not in the customer's hands; "free transfer" corresponds to the crucial factual basis of "illegal possession intent." Don't just focus on on-chain evidence: bank statements, recharge records, platform promotional promises, profit-sharing mechanisms, chat logs, contract terms, and backend screenshots are often more persuasive to law enforcement than "on-chain analysis reports." It's also crucial to explain the probability of recovery beforehand: a contract backdoor + cross-chain + anonymity structure essentially maximizes the difficulty of asset recovery; even criminal charges may not "save" the assets, but at least control of key nodes can be secured. The Core Reasons for Obstruction of Civil Remedies Looking back at the three cases mentioned above, it can be seen that although the types of cases are different, they all encountered highly similar institutional obstacles in the civil path after entering the remedy stage. 1. The Principle of Criminal Priority Acts involving criminal offenses must first be investigated and prosecuted; civil litigation usually has to wait for the completion of criminal proceedings. Once a criminal judgment addresses property rights, a subsequent civil lawsuit will trigger the principle of "res judicata." If a civil case is transferred to the public security bureau and the court determines that the conduct constitutes a crime, the civil path is forcibly interrupted. 2. Difficulty in Cross-Border Accountability

  • The funds and suspects involved in the case are distributed across multiple countries, making cross-border evidence collection, investigation, and enforcement highly restricted.

  • Anonymity and programmability allow assets to be split and transferred in a short period of time, further weakening the possibility of recovery.

3. Complex Asset Identification and Behavior Characterization

  • Crypto assets can be payment tools or carry investment or derivative rights; differences in characterization directly affect the path to relief.

  • Even if civil proceedings are initiated, courts often find it difficult to support requests for restitution due to asset dispersion, insufficient evidence, or unclear application of law.

Practical Implications

Limited civil remedies are not only a procedural issue, but also a systemic constraint.

In currency-related cases, the criminal route remains the most realistic and feasible remedy. The core role of lawyers is to help clients rationally plan their path and avoid exhausting their only remedy in procedural choices.

Implications for lawyers: Don't just "pile up materials," focus on "path control."

Based on the issues exposed at different stages of the three cases mentioned above, the core capabilities of lawyers in currency-related cases can be summarized into three levels: front-end risk identification, evidence and structural control during the process, and a clear understanding of the boundaries of the system.

(I) Front-end: Identifying risks in advance, rather than remediating afterward. Legality assessment of transactions: Analyze whether they involve risks of illegal fundraising, fraud, or illegal business operations, focusing on whether the tokens have security characteristics. Asset attribute differentiation: Payment or functional tokens are more likely to be included in the "tradable property" framework; tokens with guaranteed returns are more likely to trigger criminal intervention. Cross-border structure prediction: Whether it involves overseas entities, exchanges, or wallet addresses directly determines the difficulty of subsequent accountability. (II) Process: Constructing a Judicially Acceptable Chain of Evidence Standardization of Contracts and Transaction Records: Clearly define the purpose of transactions and rights and obligations; systematically store on-chain transaction records and operation logs. Retention of Cross-Border Communication Evidence: Bank statements, platform emails, chat logs, etc.; attention should be paid to the time of formation and continuity. (III) Understanding Institutional Boundaries: Path Selection Itself is a Strategy Most cryptocurrency-related cases cannot bypass criminal procedures; the feasibility of criminal proceedings should be assessed first. Civil litigation is more of a supplementary tool for negotiation and fund recovery than a primary reliance. Lawyers need to clearly manage client expectations and avoid misinterpreting "procedure existence" as "inevitable outcome." (IV) Advanced Practice: Towards "Critical Judgments" Many cases may still get stuck even if you prepare the materials to the best of your ability. The reason is usually not the level of effort, but several "critical judgments": How exactly is the nature of the behavior understood (fraud vs. dispute; organizer vs. intermediary; beneficiary vs. tool); Can the risk structure be identified in advance (profit promises, collection nodes, transaction entry points, evidence of control); Have the window of opportunity been seized (freezing nodes, investigation nodes, entity locking nodes)? These judgments aren't written in the legal statutes, but they determine the course of a case. From "What can be done" to "When can it be done": Turning key judgments into reusable methods. Returning to the three cases mentioned earlier, a common thread emerges: Many cryptocurrency cases aren't "without rules," but rather there are gaps between the rules—characterization, jurisdiction, evidence, asset control—each of these can potentially halt the case's progress. More realistically, even if the lawyer prepares the materials perfectly, the case may still stall at a certain point—not due to a lack of ability, but because it encounters several types of "critical judgments": Is this fraud or a transaction dispute? Can the responsible party be identified? Is the asset control window still open? These judgments are difficult to explain with a single sentence of experience, nor can they be directly derived from a few legal provisions. It's more like a "craft" of case handling: the same facts can be presented in different ways – some can present them as grounds for prosecution, while others can only describe them as "suspected disputes." The difference often lies in how the evidence is organized, how the path is ordered, and how the key points are accurately identified. Therefore, the closed-door seminar/practical training we arranged in Zhengzhou this year wasn't about "repeating the concepts," but rather about breaking down these judgments and transforming them into working methods that lawyers can directly use: Focusing on the most easily misjudged and outcome-influencing points: when to prioritize criminal proceedings, when to pursue asset preservation, when to use civil litigation as a supplementary tool, and when to adjust strategies. Systematically dissecting the criminal entry points, jurisdictional leverage, asset control nodes, and the organization of the evidence loop; Clearly explaining the basic logic of the industry and transaction system, and also creating a reusable framework for the high-risk structures in cryptocurrency cases, the application of charges and defense approaches, and the methodologies for key case progression points. The goal is simple—to get you back into practice, able to handle consultations, and more reliably handle cryptocurrency criminal cases.

data.title: Crypto Trends in 2026: New Battlegrounds for Privacy, Decentralization, and Security
data.description:
Privacy will become the most important moat for the crypto world this year

Privacy is a key function for truly putting global finance on-chain. Unfortunately, almost all blockchains currently lack this feature. For most chains, privacy has always been an optional extra. Today, privacy alone is enough to make a chain stand out from the crowd.

More importantly, privacy can create an on-chain "lock-in effect," forming a unique network effect. This is especially true today, when performance advantage is no longer the sole competitive advantage.

If everything is public, migrating from one chain to another via cross-chain protocols is almost effortless. But when it comes to privacy, the situation is different: tokens can cross chains, but "secrets" are difficult to cross chains. There is always a risk of leakage when moving from a privacy chain to a public chain, or even between two privacy chains; for example, metadata such as transaction time and size may be tracked by external observers.

Compared to new chains that are highly homogenized and whose transaction fees may be driven to zero, blockchains with privacy features can have stronger network effects. The reality is that if a "general-purpose chain" lacks a mature ecosystem, killer applications, or distribution advantages, few people will choose to use or develop on it, let alone cultivate loyalty. On public chains, cross-chain transactions are easy for users; joining any chain makes little difference. However, on privacy chains, choosing which chain to join is crucial—once joined, users are unlikely to easily leave to avoid exposing themselves. This creates a "winner-takes-all" scenario. Because most real-world applications require privacy, a few privacy chains may control most of the value in the crypto world. This year's challenges for instant messaging applications are not just about resisting quantum computing, but also about decentralization. With the approach of quantum computing, many encryption-based communication applications (Apple, Signal, WhatsApp) are performing well. The problem is that every mainstream communication application relies on private servers we trust, servers that are easily shut down by governments, have backdoors implanted, or are forced to hand over data. What's the use of quantum encryption if a country can shut down servers directly, or if companies control the private server keys? Private servers mean "you have to trust me," while the absence of private servers means "you don't have to trust me." Communication doesn't need a company as an intermediary; we need open protocols, where we don't have to trust anyone. The way to achieve this is through decentralized networks: no private servers, no single application, all code is open source, and top-tier encryption (including quantum threat protection). In an open network, no individual, company, institution, or country can deprive us of our ability to communicate. Even if an application is shut down, hundreds of new versions will emerge the next day; shutting down a node will be immediately replaced by economic incentives (mechanisms like blockchain). When users control their messages like they control their own funds—possessing private keys—everything changes. Applications can come and go, but users always control their messages and identities. Ultimately, users truly own their messages, not just the applications themselves. This isn't just a matter of quantum encryption; it's a matter of "ownership" and "decentralization." Without these two elements, we've simply built an "unbreakable" encryption system that can be shut down at any time. "Privacy as a Service"—Making Privacy Infrastructure. Behind every model, intelligent agent, and automated system lies data. But today, most data pipelines—the data input to models or the outputs—are opaque, tamper-proof, and unauditable. This may not be a problem for some consumer applications, but industries like finance and healthcare require sensitive data to be kept confidential. This is also a significant obstacle for institutions looking to put real-world assets on-chain. So, how can we ensure privacy while making innovation secure, compliant, autonomous, and globally interoperable? Many approaches are being explored, with the author focusing on "data access control": Who controls sensitive data? How does data flow? Who or what can access it? Without access control, confidential data can only rely on centralized services or self-built systems—time-consuming, expensive, and hindering traditional financial institutions from fully utilizing on-chain data. As intelligent systems begin to browse, trade, and make decisions autonomously, users and institutions need cryptographic guarantees, not just "best-effort" trust. This is why we need "privacy as a service": providing programmable data access rules, client-side encryption, decentralized key management, specifying who can decrypt, under what conditions, and for how long… all rules are enforced on-chain. By combining verifiable data systems, privacy services can become a core public infrastructure of the internet, rather than just application-layer privacy patches, truly making privacy an infrastructure. Security testing will shift from "code is law" to "standards are law." Last year, even DeFi protocols with experienced teams, rigorous audits, and years of operational experience suffered hacking attacks. This exposed a disturbing fact: current security practices are still primarily experience-based and case-driven. This year, DeFi security needs to move from "vulnerability models" to "design-level attributes," and from "best-effort" to "principle-driven": Static/Pre-deployment (testing, auditing, formal verification): Systematically verify global invariants, rather than manually selected local conditions. Teams are now using AI tools to assist in proving and proposing invariants, significantly reducing manual costs. Dynamic/Post-Deployment (Runtime Monitoring, Execution-Time Enforcement): These invariants can be transformed into real-time guardrails; every transaction must meet the rules, otherwise it automatically rolls back. This means no longer assuming every vulnerability can be pre-discovered, but rather enforcing critical security attributes directly in the code. This is not just theoretical. In fact, to date, almost all known vulnerabilities trigger these checks at runtime, potentially preventing hacking attacks. Therefore, the once-popular "code is law" concept will evolve into "standards are law": even new attacks must meet system security attributes, leaving the remaining attacks either minor or extremely difficult to execute.

data.title: Who is the true god of Polymarket?
data.description:
Authors: Claire, Morris, Sunny, Hubble AI

Polymarket boasts numerous "god-like" addresses with single-trade profits exceeding $100,000. However, faced with hundreds of thousands of accounts, a core question consistently plagues participants: Is this replicable alpha, or unsustainable luck?

Existing leaderboards have a serious blind spot: they only show short-term results, failing to reveal the stability of strategies. To eliminate the element of luck, we bypassed simple leaderboards and directly analyzed 90,000 active addresses and 2 million settled transactions on-chain.

After removing the interference of floating profits, we discovered four brutally real "counterintuitive" patterns in the prediction market and redefined the selection criteria for copy trading.

TL;DR

  • Mid-frequency efficiency trap: The most active retail trading group (mid-frequency), although having the highest win rate across the entire network, is limited by capital efficiency and a lack of systemic advantages, resulting in a median actual return approaching zero.

  • Certainty trap: Betting on high-probability events (>0.8) faces an extremely asymmetrical risk-reward ratio (a small profit if you win, zero if you lose), with a negative long-term expected value.

  • Golden odds range: True alpha is highly concentrated in the price range of 0.2 - 0.4. This is the area with the greatest market divergence and the optimal profit-loss ratio (Odds).

  • Focus premium: Data proves that "all-round" traders struggle to survive.

  • Vertical experts who specialize in a few specific sectors typically earn four times the average return of diversified traders. Finding 1: Mid-frequency trading carries the highest risk. We categorize addresses into three tiers based on the number of trades: Low-frequency trading (Low): ~0.35 trades per day | Win rate ~40% Mid-frequency trading (Mid): ~3.67 trades per day | Win rate ~43% High-frequency/Ultra-frequency trading (High/Ultra): >14 trades per day | Win rate ~21-26% Superficially, mid-frequency traders seem to be the market leaders: highest win rate: reaching ~43%; lowest percentage of losing accounts: Loss Ratio only... 50.3%, far lower than the High group's 77.1%. This gives the illusion that as long as you maintain a moderate number of 3-4 trades per day, you can consistently profit. However, when we introduce PnL (Profit/Loss) data, the truth comes to light: Median PnL: The value for the median group is 0.001, almost zero. What does this mean? It means that for the vast majority of median traders, despite daily research, betting, and seemingly winning more than losing, your account equity remains stagnant. In contrast, while High Frequency (HF) and Ultra Frequency (UHF) have median losses (-0.30 and -1.76 respectively), their Mean Profit/Loss (MPL) is boosted to +922 or even +2717 by a very small number of top-performing addresses. This illustrates that the HF field is a "battleground for machines"—surviving on low win rates, high profit/loss ratios, and systematic strategies (such as market making and arbitrage), a model that ordinary people cannot replicate. Deep Attribution: Why Does Mid-Frequency Trading Fall into the "Mediocrity Trap"? Lacking Systematic Alpha, Becoming a "Coin Flipper": Mid-frequency traders are mostly active retail investors. A win rate of ~43% and a median return close to 0 indicate that the overall performance of this group is close to a random walk. They participate in the market based on intuition or fragmented information. While this avoids the significant drawdowns caused by strategy failures seen in high-frequency bots, it also fails to build a true competitive advantage. They are repeatedly participating in the market, rather than profiting from it. Survivorship bias masks tail risks: there is a huge gap between the average PnL (+915) and the median PnL (-0.001) of mid-frequency traders. This indicates extreme polarization within the mid-frequency group. A very small number of "experts" with core insider knowledge or exceptional judgment raise the average, while the remaining 50% or more are wasting their time. High-frequency trading is difficult to learn, and low-frequency trading is insufficient: ordinary users cannot imitate the systematic high-frequency strategies of the High/Ultra band (high technical threshold, low win rate, and high psychological pressure), yet they are unwilling to accept the extremely low trading frequency of the Low band. As a result, a large amount of capital and energy has been poured into the Mid-frequency band, making it the most crowded, fiercely competitive, and mediocre "red ocean." Practical Insight: Data tells us a harsh truth: if you simply become a "diligent mid-frequency trader," the result is likely to be a wasted effort. The real value lies not in imitating the average behavior of "mid-frequency" traders, but in identifying differences. Avoiding Pitfalls: The vast majority of mid-frequency addresses are simply performing Brownian motion, offering no copy trading value. Mining: The real Alpha is hidden at the right tail of the mid-frequency group—those extremely few who, at the same frequency, outperformed the "zero gravity." This is precisely the core value of our copy trading tool: helping you skip the trial-and-error phase of "long-term mid-frequency trading with no advantage," and using algorithms to directly identify the 1% of Alpha addresses that truly generate excess returns from a massive amount of mediocre mid-frequency denominators. Discovery Two: "Picking up pennies" and "buying lottery tickets" will eventually lead to nothing. We stratified traders' risk preferences according to their holding prices and discovered a harsh reality: whether you only buy "lottery tickets" (<0.2) or only buy "certainty" (>0.9), you will be a loser in the long run. We define three typical strategies: High-certainty strategy (Consensus Betting): Positions are concentrated at prices >0.9, focusing on events that are "almost certain." High-odds strategy (Long-shot Betting): Positions are concentrated at prices <0.2, betting on low-probability, low-risk events. Dynamic strategy: Positions are evenly distributed, not fixated on extreme odds. The data reveals a huge profit gap: Data Interpretation: The average return of hybrid strategies is 13 times that of high-certainty strategies. It's worth noting that the median return for all groups is ≤0. This means that even in the best-performing hybrid group, profits are highly concentrated in the hands of top players, and the vast majority of people do not even beat transaction fees. 1. Why does betting on "certainty" fail? Intuitively, buying into a "sure-win" strategy with a 0.95 bet seems to have very low risk. From a financial mathematics perspective, this is an extremely poor trade: **Extremely Asymmetric Downside Risk:** Entering at 0.95 means risking 1.0 of your principal for a 0.05 return. If a black swan event occurs (e.g., Biden suddenly withdrawing from the race, or a game being reversed in the last minute), the loss from a single event would require 19 consecutive successful trades to break even. In the long run, the probability of a black swan event is often higher than 5%. **Alpha Exhaustion (Priced In):** When the price reaches >0.9, market consensus has already formed. Entering at this point is essentially taking over for those who are already aware of the market's moves; there is no informational advantage to be gained. 2. The "Lottery Trap" of High-Odds Strategies Betting on low-probability events with odds <0.2 also performs poorly because: Overestimation Bias: Retail investors often overestimate their ability to predict "unpopular" events. In efficient predictive markets, prices usually already contain most of the implicit information. Long-term purchases of "lottery tickets" that are correctly priced by the market will inevitably result in the continuous erosion of capital. Low Capital Efficiency: Although the profit multiple per trade is high, the extremely low win rate will cause capital to remain in a state of drawdown for a long time, making it difficult to form a compounding effect. Actionable Insight: Reject "one-track mind" traders. When selecting copy trading targets, avoid addresses with extreme price distributions (all red or all green). True Alpha players are characterized by strategic flexibility—they bet on divergence at 0.3 and take profits at 0.8, rather than mechanically sticking to a particular odds range. Finding 3: The optimal risk range is 0.2-0.4. We stratified addresses by average purchase cost (Implied Probability) to find the "sweet spot" with the highest risk-adjusted returns. The data reveals a clear non-linear return distribution: true Alpha doesn't exist at the extremes, but is concentrated in the 0.2-0.4 price range. Comparison of performance across different price ranges: [link to image] In-depth insight: Why is 0.2-0.4 the most profitable range? 1. Capturing "Pricing Divergence" A buy price between 0.2 and 0.4 indicates that the market consensus believes the probability of the event occurring is only 20%-40%. Traders who consistently profit within this range are essentially engaging in "cognitive arbitrage." They can identify events underestimated by public sentiment (e.g., the market is overly pessimistic and misjudging a candidate's chances of a comeback). Compared to simply following the consensus (buying > 0.8), betting in the divergence zone, once validated, can yield explosive returns of 2.5 to 5 times. 2. The Perfect "Asymmetric Risk/Reward Structure" In the > 0.8 range (certainty trap): Investors face poor odds of "a small profit if they win, zero if they lose." As the data shows, the average return in this range is negative, and the win rate is only 19.5% (meaning that most people who buy > 0.8 ultimately perish due to black swan events).

  • In the 0.2-0.4 range (Alpha comfort zone): This is a range with "convexity." Downside risk is locked (principal), while upside potential is flexible. Excellent traders maximize profits in this range through the dual advantages of a high win rate (49.7%) and high odds.

  • Avoid the "lottery trap" (<0.2): Although the theoretical odds are highest in the extremely low price range, data shows that its performance is far inferior to the 0.2-0.4 range. This indicates that events below 0.2 are often true "garbage time" or pure noise, and excessively betting on extremely low probability events lacks statistically positive expected value.

Practical Insight: Pay attention to "divergence hunters".

Actionable Insight: Focus on "divergence hunters".

When selecting traders to copy, priority should be given to those whose average purchase price has consistently remained between 0.2 and 0.4. This data characteristic indicates that the account neither blindly chases high-risk lotteries nor tries to "pick up pennies" in low-odds consensus zones, but rather focuses on finding undervalued opportunities where market pricing has become ineffective. This is the core competency most worth replicating. Finding 4: Concentrated strategies outperform diversified strategies. We calculated the Focus Ratio (total number of transactions / number of markets participated in) for each address and categorized them into two types: Diversified strategy: Participates in a large number of markets, with fewer transactions per market. Concentrated strategy: Focuses on a few markets, with more transactions per market. Results show: The concentrated strategy yielded four times the return of the diversified strategy ($1,225 vs $306). It's worth noting that the concentrated strategy actually had a lower win rate ($33.8% vs 41.3%). The concentrated strategy achieved significant returns on a small number of high-odds opportunities. Explanation: In-depth research creates an advantage. Focusing on researching a few markets makes it easier to identify market pricing discrepancies, thus generating excess returns on a few trades. Win rate is not the key metric. What matters is not the number of wins, but the ratio of winning profits to losing losses. Concentrated strategies accept a lower win rate in exchange for higher returns per trade. Limitations of Diversification: Participating in too many markets leads to insufficient research depth in each market, making one more susceptible to market consensus and hindering the discovery of true alpha. Analogy: As Warren Buffett said, "Diversification is the self-protection of the ignorant." If one has an informational or judgmental advantage, one should concentrate on the few opportunities with the highest certainty. Lesson from Copy Trading: Prioritize traders who focus on specific types of markets (such as specific sports leagues, political events in specific countries, etc.). Their level of specialization often implies a deeper understanding and stronger predictive ability. II. Lessons for Copy Traders: How to Identify Real "Smart Money"? To quantify the level of specialization among traders, we constructed the Focus Ratio (Focus Ratio = Total Number of Trades / Number of Markets Participated) and divided traders into two distinct groups: **Generalists:** Participating broadly in a large number of markets, with low frequency of trades in a single market, attempting to reduce risk through diversification. **Specialists:** Focusing deeply on a few markets, repeatedly trading and adding to positions in a single market, exhibiting a strong "sniping" characteristic. The data reveals a striking "focus premium": Strategy Type Average Return (Avg PnL) Win Rate Number of Addresses Generalists $306 41.3% 68,016 Specialists $1,225 33.8% 22,458 Data Interpretation: The average return of concentrated strategies is 4 times that of diversified strategies. However, a highly misleading phenomenon emerges: the win rate of concentrated strategies (33.8%) is significantly lower than that of diversified strategies (41.3%). This reveals the true profit logic of advanced players in the prediction market. In-depth Attribution: Why "Less is More"? 1. Building a Moat Through Information Asymmetry (Information Edge) Prediction markets are essentially information games. Decentralized traders attempt to span multiple fields such as politics, sports, and crypto, resulting in a superficial understanding of any single market, making them vulnerable to exploitation. Centralized traders, on the other hand, cultivate a vertical information advantage by focusing on a single sector (e.g., studying only NBA player data or tracking polls in swing states). This depth allows them to detect subtle market pricing discrepancies. 2. Debunking the Win-Rate Fallacy Data shows that high returns are often accompanied by relatively low win rates. This is because concentrated experts tend to act when there are high odds/high divergence (e.g., buying at odds of 0.3), rather than picking up "certainty coins" > 0.9. Diversified: Wins many small bets (high win rate), loses one big bet (black swan event), ultimately resulting in mediocre returns. Concentrated: Can tolerate many small trial-and-error attempts (low win rate) in exchange for a few precise, high-risk, high-reward trades. This is typical venture capital (VC) logic, not the logic of working for someone else. 3. Validating Buffett's Logic in the Prediction Market As Buffett said, "Diversification is the self-protection of the ignorant." In the stock market, diversification is to avoid unsystematic risks; however, in the zero-sum game of the prediction market, diversification often means the dilution of attention. If you are confident that you have some edge, the best strategy is not to cast a wide net, but to concentrate your firepower on the few opportunities you are most confident in. Practical Insight: Find "vertical track experts." In copy trading, a high Focus Ratio is a more important indicator than a high win rate. Bad Signal: Avoid those "jacks-of-all-trades" who buy everything. Good Signal: Prioritize accounts active only under specific tags. For example, an address that only trades "US Election" and has a stable profit curve is far more valuable than an address that trades both "NBA" and "Bitcoin." The level of specialization directly determines the purity of the Alpha. III. From Data Insights to Practical Tools This report is not only a data review but also the underlying logic for building our Smart Copy-Trading system. To achieve long-term profitability on Polymarket, manually screening 90,000 addresses is unrealistic. We are encapsulating these exclusive data insights into an automated screening and risk control tool to solve the three most challenging problems in copy trading: 1. Intelligent Removal of Market Maker Noise: Current public rankings are riddled with market makers (MMs) and arbitrage bots that inflate trading volumes. Copying them not only fails to generate profits but can also lead to losses due to slippage. Solution: Utilizing proprietary order book analysis and trading characteristic recognition algorithms, we automatically filter out systematic market makers, focusing only on active traders who genuinely profit based on their insights. 2. Vertical Matching Based on "Focus": General "profit rankings" have limited value; you need experts in specific fields. Solution: Based on Focus Ratio and historical behavior, we assign high-precision "capability tags" (such as US elections, NBA sports events, and Crypto whales) to addresses. The system will accurately match you with vertical experts with informational advantages in the fields you follow. 3. Dynamic Style Drift Monitoring (Style Drift Detection) The most insidious risk of copy trading lies in the sudden failure of a trader's strategy or a sudden change in behavior. Solution: We have established a real-time risk control model. When a long-term stable address suddenly deviates from its historical behavioral characteristics (e.g., from low-frequency focus to high-frequency broad-based trading, or an abnormally large single risk exposure), the system will identify it as an abnormal signal and issue a timely warning to help users avoid drawdown risks. Conclusion & Product Beta Testing Prediction markets are a brutal zero-sum game. Data from 90,000 addresses proves that long-term winners succeed because they are extremely disciplined: focusing on specific areas and identifying pricing discrepancies. All the core metrics mentioned in this report (Focus Ratio, pricing range analysis, market maker elimination) are integrated into Hubble's data backend. Our initial motivation for building this tool was simple: to replace blind retail investor intuition with an institutional-grade data perspective. Beta Testing Application: Hubble's Polymarket intelligent copy trading tool is currently undergoing small-scale gray-scale testing. If you agree with the above data analysis logic and would like to experience this product:

  • Like/share to support this content;

  • Leave a comment with "Waitlist";

We will send you a beta testing invitation via private message. We hope this data-driven selection system can help you truly outperform the market.

(Data Note: This study is based on settled transaction data from the Polymarket platform since its launch. All conclusions are derived from Hubble's exclusive on-chain PnL algorithm analysis.)

data.title: PayPal Joins Google’s AI-Powered Checkout as Key Payment Option Through Universal Commerce Protocol
data.description:
PayPal To Become Key Payment Option In Google’s New AI-Driven Checkout

PayPal has confirmed it will integrate with Google’s Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), an AI-powered framework designed to streamline interactions between consumers, retailers, and payment providers.

The company will soon appear as a payment choice within Google’s new checkout system, according to a press statement released over the weekend.

Excited to share @PayPal's support of @Google's Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP) as the next phase of our expanded partnership announced last fall.

This is a milestone moment making agentic commerce a reality for consumers.

Let's go @sundarpichai! 🚀 pic.twitter.com/9QCZo04n67

— Alex Chriss (@acce) January 12, 2026

How Will UCP Change Online Shopping

Google unveiled UCP during the National Retail Federation’s annual convention in New York, positioning it as an open, agentic commerce standard.

The protocol is designed to allow AI systems to assist with product discovery, checkout, and post-purchase support while enabling merchants and payment providers to interact seamlessly.

Vidhya Srinivasan, vice president and general manager of Google Ads & Commerce, explained:

“Instead of requiring unique connections for every individual agent, UCP enables all agents to interact easily.”

The protocol is platform-agnostic, aiming to reduce one-off integrations and support multiple credential providers.

UCP builds on Google’s previous agentic commerce protocols, including Agent Payments Protocol (AP2), Agent2Agent, and Model Context Protocol (MCP), and the company plans to expand its capabilities with loyalty programs, personalised recommendations, and tailored shopping experiences.

We’ve launched the Universal Commerce Protocol (UCP), a new open standard for agentic commerce that works across the shopping journey!

UCP is compatible with A2A, AP2, and MCP, and was co-developed with partners like Etsy, Shopify, Wayfair, and Target → https://t.co/0kpbdUyKj0pic.twitter.com/shuBlZho83

— Google Cloud Tech (@GoogleCloudTech) January 12, 2026

Why PayPal Is Joining UCP

PayPal executives described the protocol as a way to remove barriers that have limited agentic commerce adoption.

Prakhar Mehrotra, SVP and Head of AI at PayPal, said:

“Protocols like UCP turn agentic commerce into something merchants can actually adopt at scale. Interoperability is what allows retailers to connect at once and reach many environments, while maintaining trust, transparency, and control.”

Michelle Gill, general manager of small business and financial services at PayPal, added:

“The next generation of commerce will be defined by how well we build open, trusted infrastructure that serves everyone. Supporting and collaborating with Google on UCP shows how a trusted payments experience layer makes agentic commerce a reality for consumers.”

PayPal has long acted as a reliable infrastructure layer for online payments, connecting hundreds of millions of consumers and tens of millions of merchants globally, while offering fraud protection and buyer-seller safeguards.

The company believes these foundations will be increasingly important as AI agents take on larger roles in shopping.

Who Helped Build UCP

UCP was developed with input from major retailers and e-commerce platforms, including Shopify, Etsy, Wayfair, Target, and Walmart.

AI agents will be a big part of how we shop in the not-so-distant future.

To help lay the groundwork, we partnered with Shopify, Etsy, Wayfair, Target and Walmart to create the Universal Commerce Protocol, a new open standard for agents and systems to talk to each other across… pic.twitter.com/8gDxDWjF9q

— Sundar Pichai (@sundarpichai) January 11, 2026

Vanessa Lee, vice president at Shopify, highlighted the company’s experience in building large-scale checkout systems:

“Shopify has a history of building checkouts for millions of unique retail businesses. We have taken everything we’ve seen over the decades to make UCP a robust commerce standard that can scale.”

Some analysts caution that agentic commerce may not succeed without active coordination between consumers, merchants, and payment providers.

Richard Crone, CEO of Crone Consulting, warned that if checkouts move off merchant sites, “the merchant loses the last touch point…The product detail pages are the fuel that they need to feed their agentic commerce engine.”

PayPal Expands Its European Presence

Alongside its UCP integration, PayPal has participated in a €12 million Series A funding round for Amsterdam-based payments firm Klearly, bringing the company’s total funding to €20 million.

Klearly serves over 4,000 merchants and plans to expand deeper into Italy and Belgium.

A year after €6m seed led via former @Mastercard CEO, Dutch hospitality #payments#startup#Klearly raises €12m Series A led by @PayPal's VC arm. Just 3 years old, offers agile, bar/restaurant focussed #payment layer that is hardware/ePOS agnostic. https://t.co/UPhZ9XeZ1mpic.twitter.com/7Gepx9FxIa

— Rob Fernandes (@rob2775) January 13, 2026

In the United States, PayPal has strengthened its physical presence in New York City with a 10-year lease covering 261,000 square feet at 345 Hudson Street and 555 Greenwich Street in Hudson Square, near Google and Disney offices.

345 Hudson Street (left) and 555 Greenwich Street (right) in Hudson Square

Will UCP Redefine Commerce Infrastructure

PayPal sees UCP as a chance to enhance trust and efficiency in the emerging agentic commerce landscape.

Ashish Gupta, VP/GM of Merchant Shopping at Google, stated:

“For agentic commerce to scale, it’s critical for the industry to align on a common set of standards. We are proud to have PayPal endorse the Universal Commerce Protocol as the foundation for that future.”

With the new integration, AI-driven shopping experiences could become more seamless, giving consumers smarter product suggestions while allowing merchants and payment providers to maintain oversight and reliability.

data.title: Google poached Apple, delivering another crushing defeat for OpenAI.
data.description:
At dawn on January 13th, Beijing time, Apple and Google jointly released a statement announcing a multi-year, in-depth cooperation agreement: the next generation of Apple Foundation Models will be built on Google Gemini models and cloud technology, providing core technical support for the personalized Siri that will be launched this year.

According to the agreement, the cooperation between the two parties will go beyond simple application-layer access.

Google's Gemini model will directly become the building block of Apple Intelligence, especially for complex tasks requiring cloud computing power. Previously, it was reported that users would experience a fully-fledged Siri powered by Gemini in iOS 26.4, which is expected to be released as early as March or April this year. The new Siri will have cross-application information integration capabilities, able to understand screen content, emails, and chat history, truly achieving "understanding what you think." OpenAI: From "Honored Guest" to "Passerby" The most intriguing part of this statement is the name it didn't mention—OpenAI. Back in 2024, when Apple first unveiled Apple Intelligence at WWDC, OpenAI's ChatGPT was its first high-profile partner. In the demonstration at the time, Siri would ask users "Do you want to answer via ChatGPT?" when faced with a difficult question. However, two years later, when Apple decided to build a deeper, next-generation foundational model, it ultimately chose Gemini. This has been interpreted by many overseas media outlets as GPT's "exit," or at least its marginalization. Compared to the "plug-in" service offered by OpenAI, Google's solution this time is more like "internal implantation." By directly using Gemini's model architecture and cloud technology to train Apple's base models, Google successfully penetrated the heart of Apple's ecosystem, much like they did with Samsung. For OpenAI, losing its exclusive access to the iPhone's underlying system is undoubtedly a major setback in the battle for mobile traffic. However, there are two main reasons behind OpenAI's "exit": First, the asymmetry of infrastructure. OpenAI's computing power heavily relies on Microsoft Azure, while Google possesses independent computing power across the entire stack, from TPU chips to data centers. For a giant like Apple with hundreds of millions of users, directly partnering with Google, which possesses underlying computing power, offers significant advantages in terms of cost control and response speed compared to "subcontracting" through Microsoft Cloud via OpenAI. Secondly, from "ally" to "threat." OpenAI is rapidly moving towards hardware. As OpenAI begins developing its own hardware devices and continues to poach talent from Apple's hardware design team (even including the new company of former Apple design director Jony Ive), it is no longer seen by Apple as a pure software service provider, but as a potential and dangerous system-level competitor. The Boomerang of History: From the First iPhone to the Android Wars This collaboration between Apple and Google inevitably evokes memories of the "honeymoon era" of the mobile internet's dawn nearly 20 years ago. In 2007, when the first iPhone was released, Google was Apple's closest ally. The iPhone came pre-installed with native Google Maps and YouTube apps, and Google's then-CEO, Eric Schmidt, even sat on Apple's board of directors. It was a golden age of mutual complementarity: Apple provided revolutionary hardware, and Google provided killer internet services.

But this relationship quickly broke down due to the rise of Android. With Google launching its mobile operating system to compete with the iPhone, Jobs felt deeply betrayed and launched the famous "thermonuclear War," vowing to destroy Android.

For the next decade or so, the two companies clashed fiercely in various battlegrounds, including app stores, map services, and privacy policies. Apple not only removed Google Maps from its pre-installed list but also constantly emphasized "device-side processing" to differentiate itself from Google's "data collection" model.

In the following decade, the two companies fought tooth and nail in various battlegrounds, including app stores, map services, and privacy policies. Apple not only removed Google Maps from its pre-installed list but also constantly emphasized "device-side processing" to differentiate itself from Google's "data collection" model.

data.title: The fifth episode of the second season of "Blockchain Masters" has come to a perfect end.
data.description: On January 8, 2026, the fifth episode of the second season of "Blockchain Masters," the first light-hearted variety show in the Web3 industry, jointly launched by Jinse Finance, Twinkle, and Dashu Finance, and produced by HTX DAO and OracleX, officially went live. As the season finale, this episode brought together 12 guests and attracted over 100 ordinary people to participate on-site, achieving a high level of interaction and participation, thus concluding the second season's journey successfully. Against the backdrop of the ongoing evolution of the Web3 digital wave, "Blockchain Masters" consistently avoids complex jargon and grand narratives, using a light-hearted variety show approach and a lighter, more engaging content design to deeply connect technological concepts with real individuals. Since the project was launched in June 2025, the program has been constantly exploring in terms of content structure and expression. From diverse themes to innovative gameplay, it uses a narrative style that is close to the audience to continuously present the vibrant and diverse aspects of the Web3 world. With the airing of the fifth episode of Season 2, "Operation Disguise," this season has come to a temporary close. Looking back on this journey, "Blockchain Masters" has captured a warmer side of Web3 through authentic interaction and a companion-like experience, leaving room for future content expansion. In the fifth episode of the second season, "Blockchain Masters" further upgraded its format, using innovative mediums such as interactive tasks and character settings to construct a "real, transparent, and user-co-created" Web3 experimental field. Participants are not just guests, and viewers are not just spectators, but become part of the content through interaction, discussion, and consensus. The conclusion of the second season marks an important milestone for "Blockchain Masters" as it enters a new phase. Currently, the program has officially entered the preparation and recruitment stage for the third season. The new chapter will continue the IP's core advantages of being "lightweight, immersive, and therapeutic," while unlocking richer thematic designs, more open participation mechanisms, and deeper user co-creation, continuously exploring more possibilities for Web3 variety show expression. About Us: As a leading global blockchain industry service platform, Jinse Finance covers the entire industry ecosystem, including information, data, research reports, and summits, always staying at the forefront of the industry. This foray into film and television aims to convey the topic of Web3 in popular language and promote its wider dissemination within the industry. Twinkle is an innovative entertainment content platform focusing on Web3 themes. With keen industry insights, it captures the unique pulse of the Web3 world and transforms it into widely appealing entertainment works using professional film and television industry standards. Twinkle believes that entertainment is the best bridge, connecting cutting-edge exploration of Web3 with the curiosity of mainstream audiences. "The Dark Amber: The Crypto Reef" showcases Twinkle's strength in the "Web3 + Suspense Entertainment" direction, aiming to deliver a hardcore yet thrilling audiovisual feast. As a pioneer in Web3 entertainment, Twinkle not only builds platforms but also creates content. Twinkle excels at using professional production processes and creative entertainment techniques to uncover and present compelling stories that embody the core spirit of Web3 (such as decentralization, innovation, and value consensus). It is committed to transforming complex technical issues into captivating and resonant cinematic entertainment experiences. "The Dark Amber: The Crypto Reef" is a high-quality entertainment masterpiece created by Twinkle that blends fintech suspense with deep Web3 thinking, targeting a broader audience. HTX DAO, supported by the HTX exchange and the TRON ecosystem, uses HTX TOKEN as its core credential to build an integrated CeFi+DeFi hub. Adhering to the principles of "co-construction, co-governance, and co-sharing," the community participates in decision-making and shares profits, promoting the development of a free and open crypto financial ecosystem. Tree Finance, using content as a bridge and resources as a link, is committed to promoting the dissemination of blockchain value and providing partners with fertile ground for global exposure and sustainable growth. We focus on providing one-stop solutions for projects, connecting everyone from Web3 startups to every key node in the global crypto ecosystem. OracleX is the world's first decentralized collective consensus platform based on swarm intelligence, redefining the paradigm of human collective intelligence by constructing a next-generation collective intelligence capitalization protocol. OracleX builds a next-generation decision support ecosystem connecting the real world and the digital economy, becoming an indispensable predictive data protocol layer and financial derivative infrastructure in the Web3.0 value internet era. OracleX firmly believes that every contributor to the ecosystem should be a master of the ecosystem, sharing the benefits of platform development. It is not just a prediction platform, but a vibrant "predictive economy." Here, information flows freely, value is distributed fairly, and every participant is a builder and owner of this ecosystem. This is not only an innovation of traditional models, but also a profound exploration of the next generation of internet applications.

data.title: Meta Slashes Metaverse Team by 10% as Zuckerberg Bets Big on AI
data.description:
Meta Prepares For Massive Layoffs At Reality Labs

After burning $70 billion on virtual worlds, Meta signals a dramatic retreat from the metaverse as artificial intelligence takes over the company’s future.

Meta is preparing to cut roughly 10% of its metaverse workforce, dealing a major blow to the division that once defined CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s grand vision of the future. The layoffs, expected to hit Reality Labs, come as Meta aggressively redirects talent and capital toward artificial intelligence, following years of ballooning losses and disappointing user adoption.

BREAKING: META $META PLANNING TO CUT AROUND 10% OF EMPLOYEES IN ITS REALITY LABS DIVISION, PER NYT.

Reality Labs has roughly 15,000 employees, so this could mean ~1,500 layoffs.

Cuts will disproportionately affect those working on VR headsets and the metaverse. Could be… pic.twitter.com/lQArx04Yg7

— WOLF (@WOLF_Financial) January 12, 2026

According to a report by The New York Times, the job cuts could be announced as early as this week, underscoring how quickly Meta’s priorities have shifted. What was once pitched as the next evolution of the internet is now being overshadowed by the explosive rise of AI.

Reality Labs, which employs around 15,000 people, oversees Meta’s virtual and augmented reality ambitions, including Quest VR headsets, Horizon Worlds, and Horizon Workrooms. A 10% reduction would translate to roughly 1,500 job cuts, marking one of the most significant retreats yet from Meta’s metaverse bet.

The move follows mounting evidence that the division has failed to gain meaningful traction. Since its launch in 2020, Reality Labs has racked up more than $70 billion in losses, including $4.4 billion in operating losses in Q3 2025 alone.

We are still profitable after the Meta’s Reality Labs’ losses.

Imagine when we start profiting from it.👀 pic.twitter.com/Kz7ONIQ269

— CHIPCHIPGAME🏆Crypto Poker (@chipchipgame) April 25, 2024

Internally, Meta has already begun reshuffling budgets. Reports in December suggested the company was considering slashing up to 30% of metaverse spending, with those funds instead flowing into AI research, smart wearables, and neural interface technologies, including projects like the Meta Neural Band.

Meta declined to comment on the impending layoffs.

Meta’s pullback marks a stunning reversal from its 2021 rebrand, when the company renamed itself after the metaverse and promised a future built around immersive virtual worlds, digital identities, and Web3 economies.

While gaming-driven platforms like Roblox and Fortnite continue to thrive with hundreds of millions of users, most corporate and blockchain-based metaverses have struggled to attract sustained engagement. Data from DappRadar shows The Sandbox recorded just 776 unique active wallets over the past month, highlighting the gap between early hype and real-world usage.

Meta’s own flagship world, Horizon Worlds, has fared no better. Multiple reports suggest daily active users remain below 1,000, a stark contrast to the scale Zuckerberg once envisioned.

From text prompts to signature styles, creator Metacrafters reveals why Mesh Generation is your go-to generative AI tool for customizing worlds in Meta Horizon. 🛠️🎨

Try it in your mobile or VR world and drop a link! Start building 👉 https://t.co/wcCFHzVlV7

More links 👇 pic.twitter.com/5fCF0vVPHi

— Meta Horizon Developers (@MetaHorizonDevs) December 15, 2025

As enthusiasm faded, investors grew restless — and Meta’s metaverse losses became harder to justify in a market increasingly obsessed with AI.

AI wins as crypto and metaverse ambitions cool

Meta’s retreat from the metaverse mirrors its broader caution around blockchain initiatives. In 2025, shareholders overwhelmingly rejected a proposal to add Bitcoin to the company’s balance sheet, with just 0.08% of votes cast in favor. With Zuckerberg controlling 61% of voting power, the outcome was never in doubt.

🚨LATEST :

Meta Declines Bitcoin for Treasury Reserves #XRP Emerges as a Strategic Alternative 🚀🪙

RETWEET ONLY IF YOU FEEL BULLISH 🚀🪙 pic.twitter.com/ED0uNiXlot

— ToniTheRippler (@thatgirl_chichi) June 1, 2025

The message from investors has been clear: focus on what works.

That focus is now firmly on artificial intelligence. Meta has rapidly expanded investment in generative AI models, recommendation engines, AI-powered assistants, and wearable-integrated intelligence, positioning itself to compete directly with OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic.

After years of chasing virtual worlds that never reached escape velocity, Meta appears to be embracing a more immediate, revenue-driven future. The metaverse may not be dead — but at Meta, it’s no longer the main character.

data.title: The Way of Ether
data.description:
Ethereum itself must pass "walkaway test（walkaway test)". Ethereum's goal is to become the home of trustless and trust-minimized applications, whether in finance, governance, or other fields. It must support applications that are more like tools. Like a hammer, once you buy it, it's yours; not a service-oriented application, whose functionality completely fails once the developers lose interest in maintaining it (or worse, it's hacked). Even if some applications do rely on specific developers, Ethereum should minimize this dependence as much as possible and protect users as much as possible in the event of a dependency failure. However, if the underlying protocol itself depends on a particular "supply provider" for continued availability, even if that "supply provider" is merely part of the overall core developer workflow, then a true application ecosystem cannot be established. The Ethereum blockchain itself must possess the qualities we seek in Ethereum applications. Therefore, Ethereum itself must pass the "walk-out test." This means that Ethereum must reach a state where, if we wish, we can choose a "fixed (ossify) protocol." We don't necessarily have to stop improving the protocol, but we must reach a point where Ethereum's value proposition no longer strictly relies on any features that don't yet exist in the current protocol. This includes at least the following aspects: Complete quantum resistance. We should avoid falling into the trap of postponing quantum resistance to the last minute in order to squeeze out a few more years of efficiency. Individual users can choose this, but the protocol layer should not. Being able to proudly say that the Ethereum protocol, in its current form, will be cryptographically secure for the next hundred years is a goal we should strive to achieve as soon as possible and uphold with pride.

  • Scalable to a sufficiently large architecture. The protocol must be capable of scaling to thousands of transactions per second over time, especially including ZK-EVM verification and data sampling implemented via PeerDAS.. Ideally, future expansion would primarily be achieved through parameter adjustments; even better, these adjustments would not be BPO-style hard forks, but rather, like gas limits, through validator voting mechanisms. This would create a stateful architecture capable of operating for decades. This means we need to decide on and implement some form of partial statelessness and state expiration so that even decades after Ethereum starts running at thousands of TPS, we don't have to worry about synchronization, disk, or I/O demands overwhelming us. It also means forward-looking design for tree structures and storage types for long-term environments.

    • General Account Model (i.e., "Complete Account Abstraction"): Eliminate the hard-coded dependency on ECDSA signature verification in the protocol. We are certain that there is no DoS vulnerability in the pricing system, which includes both the execution layer and the ZK proof layer.

    • A time-tested economic model PoS  Based on our experience over the past five years in Ethereum's Proof-of-Stake (PoS) and even more than a decade earlier, we are confident that this model can remain decentralized for decades to come and support ETH as a trustless collateral asset (e.g., for governance-minimized ETH-collateralized stablecoins). This block-building model, resilient to centralized pressures, ensures censorship resistance even in an uncertain future environment. Ideally, we should complete these difficult but crucial tasks over the next few years, enabling virtually all subsequent innovations to be implemented through client-side optimizations and reflected in the protocol through parameter changes. Each year, we should check off at least one of these, ideally several. Doing things right the first time, based on a full understanding of what is truly right, rather than compromising half-baked fixes, maximizes Ethereum's long-term robustness at both the technical and social levels. Ethereum, all in. This is the way of Ethereum.

data.title: Microsoft Warns U.S. Companies Are Losing Ground As Chinese AI Models Gain Popularity In Emerging Economies
data.description:
China Surges Ahead In AI Race With DeepSeek While U.S. Struggles To Keep Pace

Microsoft warns that Chinese AI companies are rapidly gaining ground globally, using low-cost models and heavy state support to reach markets beyond the West.

Brad Smith, Microsoft president, highlighted DeepSeek’s rapid adoption in emerging markets, noting that China’s open-source models are now competitive and often cheaper than U.S. offerings.

“They benefit from subsidization by the Chinese government. They benefit from subsidies that enable [them] to basically undercut American companies based on price.”

Microsoft is basically sounding the alarm that China is winning the AI game in the parts of the world that matter most, developing countries in Southeast Asia, Africa and Latin America.

They're doing it the smart way too.

They are not trying to build the absolute best AI… https://t.co/5vChebh5vzpic.twitter.com/fgoHRvrFBX

— Milk Road AI (@MilkRoadAI) January 13, 2026

DeepSeek Captures Markets Where U.S. Tech Is Limited

Data from Microsoft shows that DeepSeek’s R1 model, launched last year, has quickly expanded in countries where American firms have limited access.

The company now holds 56% of the AI market in Belarus, 49% in Cuba, and 43% in Russia.

In Africa, it commands 18% of Ethiopia and 17% of Zimbabwe, driven by affordability and open access.

Source: Microsoft

The model’s open-source approach, including free usage and adaptability, has accelerated AI adoption in regions often overlooked by Western companies.

Smith said this growth reflects a broader shift in the global AI landscape.

Is Open Source Outpacing Locked Ecosystems

While U.S. companies like OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic maintain paid, controlled ecosystems, Chinese platforms such as DeepSeek and Meta’s Llama benefit from open-source accessibility.

Smith warned that without significant investment in infrastructure, data centres, and energy support, this divide could deepen global inequality.

“If we rely on private capital flows alone, I don’t think that will be sufficient to compete with a competitor that is subsidised to the degree that Chinese companies often are.”

He called for international development banks and governments to provide funding to support AI adoption in poorer regions.

Today, we released @Microsoft's newest AI Diffusion Report, and I sat down with @BDataScientist, Chief Data Scientist and Director of the AI for Good Lab, for a special edition of #ToolsAndWeapons to unpack the report’s most striking insights. It’s a conversation about… pic.twitter.com/W7IwYF0faP

— Brad Smith (@BradSmi) January 8, 2026

Bright Simons, an AI analyst from Ghana’s IMANI think-tank, said Chinese AI tools are now the go-to for many Africans.

He explained,

“Africans can’t afford very expensive solutions apart from open source, so you have to go to [Meta’s] Llama or Chinese options.”

He also noted local efforts such as Masakhane and InkubaLM, but Chinese models remain dominant in accessibility and reach.

Global AI Use Shows Wide Divide Between North And South

Microsoft’s AI Diffusion Report 2025 shows that 24.7% of people in the global north used AI by the end of 2025, compared with just 14.1% in the global south, leaving a worldwide average of 16.3%.

Source: Microsoft

Smith called the gap a “cause for concern,” warning that without targeted investment, inequality will widen further.

“American companies, as well as Western governments, cannot ignore Africa. If they do, they are closing their eyes to the future of the world more broadly, and I think that would be a grave mistake.”

Strong Models Don’t Guarantee Widespread Adoption

Even in technologically advanced countries, public use of AI does not always match technical capability.

By the end of 2025, about 28% of working-age Americans had used generative AI, placing the U.S. 24th globally.

Meanwhile, South Korea climbed from 25th to 18th, boosted by clearer AI policies, improved language support, and public engagement through cultural trends such as Ghibli-style graphics on social media.

Source: Microsoft

China’s Strategy Focuses On Accessibility And Low Costs

DeepSeek has leveraged its MIT-licensed model and free chatbot to lower both financial and technical barriers.

Adoption in North America and Europe remains limited, but the model has spread quickly across China, Russia, Iran, Cuba, Belarus, and parts of Africa, where usage rates can be two to four times higher than in many Western countries.

Smith emphasised that the U.S. retains advantages in trust and advanced chip access but warned that price competition and global reach are increasingly critical.

DeepSeek’s next major model is expected to launch just before Lunar New Year, signalling continued momentum in regions where American firms have struggled to make inroads.

data.title: Former NYC Mayor Eric Adams Leaves The Office And Started A Crypto Scam Called NYC Token?
data.description:
A post–City Hall crypto debut raises eyebrows

Just weeks after stepping down as mayor of New York City, Eric Adams has returned to the public spotlight with a controversial new crypto project: a Solana-based memecoin called “NYC Token.”

Adams announced the token on Monday through social media posts and a press appearance in Times Square, framing it as a civic-minded blockchain initiative designed to combat antisemitism, address what he described as rising anti-American sentiment, and fund education and scholarships in underserved communities.

“There are two types of Americans — those who live in New York and those who wish they could. If you can’t make it to New York, we’re going to bring New York to you.”

Amid the Times Square sideshows, Eric Adams announces his “NYC Token,” a crypto coin he says will fight antisemitism.

“I’m not taking a salary at this time,” he said of the yet to be launched coin. “Down the line, we will make the determination of doing so” pic.twitter.com/KnTTdTv6y1

— Josie Stratman (@JosieStratman) January 12, 2026

In interviews with FOX Business and local media, Adams said proceeds from the token would be directed to an unnamed nonprofit supporting educational programs, blockchain literacy, and social awareness campaigns. He positioned the project as an extension of his long-standing belief that cities should embrace blockchain technology to drive innovation.

The timing, however, immediately drew scrutiny. Adams officially left office on Jan. 1, and his successor, Zohran Mamdani, has taken a far more skeptical view of crypto and capitalism. Against that backdrop, the sudden launch of a mayor-branded token raised questions about governance, intent, and oversight — questions that intensified within minutes of the token going live.

A violent price swing and on-chain red flags

Despite the high-profile rollout, NYC Token’s market debut unraveled almost immediately. According to DEXScreener, the token fell from roughly $0.47 to around $0.10 within 30 minutes, wiping out hundreds of millions of dollars in paper value as its market capitalization collapsed from nearly $500 million to under $110 million.

A former New York Mayor launched a token called $NYC.

It was marketed as a way to fight “antisemitism and anti-Americanism.”

It reached $600 million.

Here’s what really happened 👇

Eric Adams shared $NYC on Solana, and people assumed it was official. Big name, big city, big… pic.twitter.com/SOnjDwPZeG

— 💎 𝐖𝐋𝐅𝐈 𝐁𝐎𝐒𝐒 💎 (@KekiusElonus) January 13, 2026

Soon after, crypto analysts began flagging suspicious on-chain activity. Rune Crypto reported that wallets linked to the token’s deployment appeared to have removed more than $3.4 million in liquidity near the market peak.

Blockchain analytics firm Bubblemaps echoed those concerns, identifying a wallet connected to the deployer that withdrew roughly $2.5 million in USDC, then later added back about $1.5 million after the price had already dropped more than 60%.

Bubblemaps described the pattern as reminiscent of prior controversial launches where liquidity was heavily manipulated, intensifying accusations that the NYC Token may have been “rugged.” Another analyst warned that the project appeared highly centralized and carried elevated risk.

🚨RUGPULL CALL - #NYC

Neither Adams nor the project’s team has publicly addressed the allegations. Requests for comment from multiple outlets had not received responses at the time of publication.

Opaque tokenomics, political optics, and unanswered questions

Beyond the market turmoil, the project’s lack of transparency has compounded concerns. The NYC Token website provides limited technical detail, with nonfunctional links to both a whitepaper and a purchase page.

While the site claims the project aims to build “a decentralized financial ecosystem as ambitious as the city itself,” it offers no roadmap, governance structure, or named partners.

Tokenomics disclosures have also raised eyebrows. According to project materials cited by multiple outlets, 70% of the total 1 billion token supply is allocated to a centralized “NYC Token Reserve,” with the remaining portion divided among liquidity, development, marketing, and team allocations. Adams has not disclosed the identities of the project’s co-founders and stated that he would not immediately draw a salary from the token.

The controversy stands in sharp contrast to Adams’ record as one of the most pro-crypto mayors in U.S. history. While in office, he accepted his paychecks in Bitcoin and Ether, hosted New York City’s first crypto summit, and promoted initiatives such as NYC Coin and a proposed BitBond — many of which never fully materialized.

Now, as New York enters a new political era under leadership openly critical of crypto, Adams’ memecoin experiment risks becoming a cautionary tale rather than a legacy project. Whether the NYC Token was a rushed civic experiment, a poorly executed launch, or something more troubling remains unclear.

What is certain is that launching a highly speculative token days after leaving public office — only to see it collapse amid rug-pull allegations — has turned a symbolic crypto play into a reputational test, not just for Eric Adams, but for the fragile intersection of politics and memecoin culture.
